voyager.escHint: Press ESC to release cursor · Exit Voyager Mode

info.emptyBody: Select a body to inspect its mission data.

flight.readyBody: The cursor will lock to the canvas. Use mouse look after launch.
flight.switchingBody: Camera and input state are being synchronized.
flight.pausedBody: Cursor control is released. Resume to lock the canvas again, or return to observatory controls.

errors.pointerLockBlockedLaunch: Pointer lock was blocked. Click the launch overlay or canvas again.
errors.pointerLockBlockedResume: Pointer lock was blocked. Click Resume Flight again.
errors.pointerLockUnsupported: This browser does not expose pointer lock for the canvas.

descriptions.unknown: Solar system body available for visual exploration.
descriptions.sun: The Sun is the hydrogen-and-helium star at the center of the solar system, holding planets, dwarf planets, and small bodies in orbit. Its light and energy make life on Earth possible.
descriptions.mercury: Mercury is the smallest planet and the closest planet to the Sun. With almost no atmosphere to retain heat, its surface swings between extreme daytime heat and deep nighttime cold.
descriptions.venus: Venus is a rocky planet similar to Earth in size and structure, but its thick atmosphere traps heat. That runaway greenhouse effect makes it the hottest planet in the solar system.
descriptions.earth: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only world known to host life. Liquid water on its surface and a stable environment support a rich biosphere.
descriptions.mars: Mars is a cold, dusty desert planet with a thin atmosphere and a red, iron-rich surface. Exploration has found evidence that ancient Mars was warmer, wetter, and wrapped in a thicker atmosphere.
descriptions.jupiter: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system, a giant world made mostly of hydrogen and helium. Its banded clouds and the Great Red Spot, a storm larger than Earth, define its appearance.
descriptions.saturn: Saturn is the second-largest planet, a gas giant made mostly of hydrogen and helium. Its bright, complex ring system and many moons make the Saturn system especially distinctive.
descriptions.uranus: Uranus is a cold, windy ice giant and the seventh planet from the Sun. Its rotation axis is tilted almost sideways, giving it some of the most unusual seasons in the solar system.
descriptions.neptune: Neptune is the most distant planet in the solar system, an ice giant far from the Sun. Despite its distance, powerful winds and changing storms drive its dynamic atmosphere.
descriptions.moon: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, orbiting about 385,000 kilometers away on average. Tidally locked to Earth, it shows us the same face while its cratered surface preserves records of solar system history.
descriptions.phobos: Phobos is the larger and inner moon of Mars, orbiting the planet three times a day. It is slowly spiraling inward and may eventually crash into Mars or break apart into a ring.
descriptions.deimos: Deimos is the smaller outer moon of Mars, completing an orbit in about 30 hours. Although heavily cratered, redeposited dust and debris give parts of its surface a smoother appearance.
descriptions.io: Io is one of Jupiter's large moons and the most volcanically active world in the solar system. Tidal forces from Jupiter and neighboring moons heat its interior and continually renew its surface.
descriptions.europa: Europa is an icy moon of Jupiter studied for the possibility of a salty ocean beneath its surface. Its fractured ice and tidal heating make it a key target for understanding potentially habitable ocean worlds.
descriptions.ganymede: Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system, even larger than Mercury. It is the only moon known to generate its own magnetic field, with evidence also pointing to an ocean beneath its icy crust.
descriptions.callisto: Callisto is Jupiter's second-largest moon, an icy and rocky world covered with ancient impact scars. Its heavily cratered surface preserves a long record of collisions with little sign of active resurfacing.
descriptions.titan: Titan is Saturn's largest moon and the only moon known to have a dense atmosphere. Its surface hosts rivers, lakes, and seas of methane and ethane, creating a liquid cycle unlike Earth's water cycle.
descriptions.enceladus: Enceladus is a small icy moon that vents water vapor and ice particles into space. Those plumes provide clues to a subsurface salty ocean and the chemical ingredients needed for habitability.
descriptions.rhea: Rhea is Saturn's second-largest moon, a cold and airless icy world. Its cratered terrain and bright fracture cliffs preserve signs of an ancient surface and past tectonic activity.
descriptions.iapetus: Iapetus is Saturn's third-largest moon, known for its stark contrast between a dark leading hemisphere and a much brighter trailing side. A high equatorial ridge also makes it one of Saturn's most distinctive moons.
descriptions.dione: Dione is an icy and rocky moon of Saturn with cratered terrain and bright fracture cliffs. Those bright ice walls point to tectonic activity in its past.
descriptions.tethys: Tethys is a cold, bright moon of Saturn made mostly of water ice. Its surface is marked by the giant Odysseus crater and the long Ithaca Chasma canyon system.
descriptions.mimas: Mimas is the smallest and innermost of Saturn's major moons, made largely of water ice. Its dominant feature is Herschel Crater, a giant impact basin spanning about a third of the moon's diameter.
descriptions.titania: Titania is Uranus' largest moon. Long fault valleys and bright deposits suggest this icy world experienced geologic activity in its past.
descriptions.oberon: Oberon is Uranus' second-largest moon, made of roughly half ice and half rock. Its old, heavily cratered surface shows little sign of internal activity.
descriptions.umbriel: Umbriel is the darkest of Uranus' large moons, with an ancient, heavily cratered surface. A bright ring-shaped feature stands out on its dark terrain, though its origin remains uncertain.
descriptions.ariel: Ariel appears to have the brightest and youngest surface among Uranus' major moons. Fault-bounded valleys cut across its terrain, pointing to relatively recent geologic activity.
descriptions.miranda: Miranda is the smallest and innermost of Uranus' five major moons, yet it has one of the most varied surfaces seen on a moon. Its coronae and deep fault canyons point to a complex geologic history.
descriptions.triton: Triton is Neptune's largest moon and the only large moon known to orbit opposite its planet's rotation. Despite its extreme cold, Voyager 2 observed active geysers and a geologically varied icy surface.
descriptions.pluto: Pluto is a Kuiper Belt dwarf planet with a complex surface of mountains, plains, glaciers, and craters. Its system includes several moons, led by the large moon Charon.
descriptions.ceres: Ceres is the largest object in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter and the only dwarf planet in the inner solar system. Spacecraft exploration has made it a key world for understanding small, water-rich bodies.
descriptions.eris: Eris is a distant dwarf planet comparable in size to Pluto, orbiting far beyond Neptune. Its discovery helped drive the redefinition that separated dwarf planets from the eight major planets.
descriptions.haumea: Haumea is an elongated dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt. Its rapid rotation shapes its unusual form, and it is known to have two moons and a ring.
descriptions.makemake: Makemake is a Kuiper Belt dwarf planet, slightly smaller than Pluto and relatively bright as seen from Earth. Its cold surface shows frozen methane and ethane, with one small provisional moon known.
